05 Custom Properties — CSS's Runtime API

← Back to lessons

CSS custom properties are not Sass variables. They're a runtime API that cascades, scopes, and can be changed dynamically with JavaScript. Understanding this difference is the key to modern theming.

The Mental Model: Variables That Participate in the Cascade

Sass variables ($color-primary) are replaced at build time. The browser never sees them. They're constants.

CSS custom properties (--color-primary) exist at runtime in the browser. They cascade. They inherit. They can change based on context. They're reactive.

Sass variables (compile time)
/* Before build: */
$primary: #a855f7;
.btn { color: $primary; }

/* After build (what the browser sees): */
.btn { color: #a855f7; }

/* The variable is GONE.
   You can't change it at runtime.
   You can't scope it to a component.
   It's a static find-and-replace. */
CSS custom properties (runtime)
/* What the browser sees: */
:root { --primary: #a855f7; }
.btn { color: var(--primary); }

/* The variable LIVES in the browser.
   Change it with JS:
   el.style.setProperty('--primary', '#22c55e')

   Scope it to a component:
   .dark-card { --primary: #e9d5ff; }

   It cascades just like any CSS property. */

Custom properties cascade. This means a --color-primary set on a parent is available to all descendants. Set it on :root for global scope. Set it on .dark-mode to override for a section. Set it on a specific component to localize. The cascade is the scoping mechanism.

Syntax, Fallbacks, and Gotchas

/* Defining custom properties */
:root {
  --color-primary: #a855f7;
  --spacing-md: 1rem;
  --font-sans: 'DM Sans', system-ui, sans-serif;
  --shadow-card: 0 4px 12px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15);
}

/* Using them with var() */
.card {
  color: var(--color-primary);
  padding: var(--spacing-md);
  font-family: var(--font-sans);
  box-shadow: var(--shadow-card);
}

/* Fallback values (used if the property doesn't exist) */
.card {
  color: var(--color-primary, #a855f7);
  /* If --color-primary isn't defined, use #a855f7 */
}

/* Nested fallbacks */
.card {
  color: var(--color-brand, var(--color-primary, purple));
  /* Try --color-brand → try --color-primary → use purple */
}

The invalid value gotcha. If a custom property resolves to an invalid value for the property using it, CSS doesn't use the fallback — it uses unset. Example: --size: red; width: var(--size); doesn't fail gracefully. The width becomes unset (auto for width), not the fallback. The variable IS defined — its value just doesn't make sense for width.

Scoping: The Power Move

Custom properties cascade, which means you can override them at any level of the DOM tree:

/* Global defaults */
:root {
  --bg: #060608;
  --text: #e8e6f0;
  --accent: #a855f7;
}

/* Override for light mode */
[data-theme="light"] {
  --bg: #f5f4f1;
  --text: #1a1918;
  --accent: #7c3aed;
}

/* Override for a specific component */
.card-danger {
  --accent: #ef4444;  /* only affects this card and its children */
}

/* All components use the SAME variables */
.card {
  background: var(--bg);
  color: var(--text);
  border-color: var(--accent);
}
.btn {
  background: var(--accent);
}

/* The .card and .btn inside .card-danger automatically get
   the red accent — no new classes needed */

This is how this platform's dark/light theme works. Look at the source: [data-theme="light"] overrides the same variables that :root defines. Every component uses var(--bg), var(--text), etc. Toggle the theme and every element updates — because they all reference the same variables, and the variables changed.

Design Tokens with Custom Properties

A token system typically has three layers:

/* Layer 1: Primitives — raw values with no semantic meaning */
:root {
  --purple-50: #faf5ff;
  --purple-500: #a855f7;
  --purple-900: #581c87;
  --gray-50: #f9fafb;
  --gray-900: #111827;
  --space-1: 0.25rem;
  --space-2: 0.5rem;
  --space-4: 1rem;
  --space-8: 2rem;
}

/* Layer 2: Semantic — meaning-based aliases */
:root {
  --color-bg: var(--gray-900);
  --color-text: var(--gray-50);
  --color-accent: var(--purple-500);
  --color-accent-subtle: var(--purple-50);
  --spacing-section: var(--space-8);
  --spacing-element: var(--space-4);
}

/* Layer 3: Component — scoped to specific components */
.card {
  --card-padding: var(--spacing-element);
  --card-bg: var(--color-bg);
  --card-border: var(--color-accent);
  padding: var(--card-padding);
  background: var(--card-bg);
  border: 1px solid var(--card-border);
}

/* Theme override only changes Layer 2 */
[data-theme="light"] {
  --color-bg: var(--gray-50);
  --color-text: var(--gray-900);
}

Tailwind v4 connection. Tailwind v4's @theme directive works exactly like this. You define tokens in @theme { } and Tailwind generates utilities from them. The underlying mechanism is CSS custom properties. Understanding this system means you understand Tailwind v4's architecture (Lesson 08).

Dynamic Theming with JavaScript

/* Read a custom property from JS */
const value = getComputedStyle(document.documentElement)
  .getPropertyValue('--accent');
// "#a855f7"

/* Set a custom property from JS */
document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--accent', '#22c55e');
/* Every element using var(--accent) updates instantly */

/* Scope it to a specific element */
card.style.setProperty('--accent', '#ef4444');
/* Only this card (and its children) see the red accent */

/* Use it for scroll-driven values */
window.addEventListener('scroll', () => {
  const pct = window.scrollY / document.body.scrollHeight;
  document.documentElement.style.setProperty('--scroll-pct', pct);
});
/* Now CSS can use var(--scroll-pct) for scroll-based effects */

Custom properties are the bridge between CSS and JavaScript. Instead of JavaScript directly manipulating styles, it sets a custom property, and CSS responds. This keeps the styling in CSS and the logic in JavaScript. Clean separation of concerns.

Advanced Patterns

Pattern 1: Component variants with custom properties

.btn {
  --btn-bg: var(--accent);
  --btn-text: white;
  --btn-padding: 0.5rem 1rem;
  background: var(--btn-bg);
  color: var(--btn-text);
  padding: var(--btn-padding);
}
.btn-outline {
  --btn-bg: transparent;
  --btn-text: var(--accent);
  border: 1px solid var(--accent);
}
.btn-lg {
  --btn-padding: 0.75rem 1.5rem;
}
/* Variants only change the variables, not the base styles */

Pattern 2: Responsive tokens without media queries

:root {
  --container-padding: 1rem;
}
@media (min-width: 768px) {
  :root { --container-padding: 2rem; }
}
@media (min-width: 1280px) {
  :root { --container-padding: 3rem; }
}
/* Every element using var(--container-padding)
   automatically responds to the viewport */

Pattern 3: Calculated values

:root {
  --base-size: 1rem;
  --scale: 1.25;
}
h1 { font-size: calc(var(--base-size) * var(--scale) * var(--scale) * var(--scale)); }
h2 { font-size: calc(var(--base-size) * var(--scale) * var(--scale)); }
h3 { font-size: calc(var(--base-size) * var(--scale)); }
/* Change --scale and the entire type hierarchy adjusts */

Check Your Understanding

1. What's the fundamental difference between Sass variables and CSS custom properties?

Sass variables are resolved at build time and disappear from the output. CSS custom properties live in the browser at runtime, cascade through the DOM, and can be changed dynamically with JavaScript.

2. How do you scope a custom property to only affect one component and its children?

Set it on that component's selector instead of :root. For example, .card-danger { --accent: #ef4444; } makes --accent red only for elements inside .card-danger.

3. What happens if var(--size, 16px) is used but --size is set to banana?

The fallback 16px is NOT used. Because --size IS defined (just with an invalid value for the context), the property becomes unset. The fallback only applies when the variable doesn't exist at all.

4. Name the three layers of a design token system.

Primitive (raw values like --purple-500), Semantic (meaning-based like --color-accent), and Component (scoped like --card-bg). Theme changes happen at the semantic layer.

5. How do you read a custom property's value from JavaScript?

getComputedStyle(element).getPropertyValue('--property-name'). Note: it reads the computed value for that specific element, respecting cascade and inheritance.

Exercise: Build a Theme Switcher

  1. Define a set of custom properties on :root for a dark theme (background, text, accent, surface)
  2. Create a [data-theme="light"] override set
  3. Build a card component that uses only these variables
  4. Add a JavaScript button that toggles the data-theme attribute
  5. Bonus: Add a third theme (e.g., "sepia") by defining a new [data-theme="sepia"] set

Recap

  1. Custom properties are runtime — they cascade, inherit, and can be changed dynamically
  2. Sass variables are compile-time — they disappear after build
  3. Scoping = setting on a selector — :root for global, .component for local
  4. Three token layers — primitive, semantic, component
  5. Theme switching overrides the semantic layer; components don't change
  6. JavaScript integration via setProperty() and getPropertyValue()
  7. Fallbacks only work when the property is undefined, not when the value is invalid
← Lesson 04: Grid Lesson 06: Modern CSS →
